title: Relate multiplication and division equations
description: See the relationship between multiplication and division equations.
topic_path: Get ready for multiplying and dividing fractions

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks to complete the table.**  
*Hint: Use the array to help you solve.*

 | | | | |
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
[[☃ input-number 2]]| $\times$ | $6$|$=$ | $18$
$18$ | $\div$ | $6$ | $=$ | [[☃ input-number 5]] 

[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
The array has $\greenD{18}$ total dots.  

[[☃ image 1]]

The dots are in $\blueD3$ rows of $\purpleC6$ dots.

We can relate the array to multiplication and division:

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$

Here is the completed table:

 | | | | |
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\fbox{\blueD{3}}$| $\times$| $\purpleC6$ | $=$ | $\greenD{18}$
$\greenD{18}$ |$\div$ | $\purpleC6$ | $=$ | $\fbox{\blueD3}$

options.alt: An array of 18 circles arranged in 3 rows of 6 circles each.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks.**  

$32 \div 8 =~$[[☃ input-number 3]]  $~~$ because  $~~$  [[☃ input-number 4]]${}~\times 8=32$.
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
How many groups of $\purpleC8$ do we need to make a total of $\greenD{32}$?

There are $\blueD4$ groups of $\purpleC8$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8}}_{\blueD{4}\purpleC{\text{ eights}}} = \greenD{32}$
$\greenD{32}\div\purpleC{8} = \fbox{\blueD{4}}$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $\fbox{\blueD4}\times\purpleC8=\greenD{32}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks.**

$48 \div 6 =~$[[☃ input-number 3]]  $~~$ because  $~~$  [[☃ input-number 4]]${}~\times 6=48$.
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
How many groups of $\purpleC6$ do we need to make a total of $\greenD{48}$?

There are $\blueD8$ groups of $\purpleC6$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6}+\purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6}}_{\blueD{8}\purpleC{\text{ sixes}}} = \greenD{48}$
$\greenD{48}\div\purpleC{6} = \fbox{\blueD{8}}$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $\fbox{\blueD8}\times\purpleC6=\greenD{48}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks.**

$14 \div{} $ [[☃ input-number 3]]  $= 7~~$ because  $~~7 \times{} $  [[☃ input-number 4]]$~=14$.
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD7$ groups, how many would need to be in each group to make a total of $\greenD{14}$?

There are $\blueD7$ groups of $\purpleC2$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{2} + \purpleC{2} + \purpleC{2} + \purpleC{2}+\purpleC{2} + \purpleC{2} + \purpleC{2}}_{\blueD{7}\purpleC{\text{ twos}}} = \greenD{14}$
$\greenD{14}\div\fbox{\purpleC{2} }= \blueD{7}$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $\blueD7\times\fbox{\purpleC2}=\greenD{14}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks.**

$9 \div{} $ [[☃ input-number 3]]  $= 9~~$ because  $~~9 \times{} $  [[☃ input-number 4]]$~=9$.
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD9$ groups, how many would need to be in each group to make a total of $\greenD{9}$?

There are $\blueD9$ groups of $\purpleC1$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{1} + \purpleC{1} + \purpleC{1} + \purpleC{1}+\purpleC{1} + \purpleC{1} + \purpleC{1}+\purpleC{1}+\purpleC{1}}_{\blueD{9}\purpleC{\text{ ones}}} = \greenD{9}$
$\greenD{9}\div\fbox{\purpleC{1}} = \blueD{9}$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $\blueD9\times\fbox{\purpleC1}=\greenD{9}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks.**

$54 \div {}$ [[☃ input-number 3]]  $= 9~~$ because  $~~9 \times {}$  [[☃ input-number 4]]$~=54$.
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD9$ groups, how many would need to be in each group to make a total of $\greenD{54}$?

There are $\blueD9$ groups of $\purpleC6$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6}+\purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6} + \purpleC{6}+\purpleC{6}+\purpleC{6} }_{\blueD{9}\purpleC{\text{ sixes}}} = \greenD{54}$
$\greenD{54}\div\fbox{\purpleC{6} }= \blueD{9}$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $\blueD9\times\fbox{\purpleC6}=\greenD{54}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks.**

[[☃ input-number 3]] ${}\div 8 =8$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $8\times8=$ [[☃ input-number 4]].
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD8$ groups of $\purpleC8$, how many total do we have?

There are $\greenD{64}$ total.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8}+\purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8}+\purpleC{8}}_{\blueD{8}\purpleC{\text{ eights}}} = \greenD{64}$
$\fbox{\greenD{64}}\div\purpleC{8} = \blueD{8}$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $\blueD8\times\purpleC8=\fbox{\greenD{64}}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks.**

[[☃ input-number 3]] $~\div$ $5=8$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $8\times5=$ [[☃ input-number 4]].
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD8$ groups of $\purpleC5$, how many total do we have?

There are $\greenD{40}$ total.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5}+\purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5}+\purpleC{5}}_{\blueD{8}\purpleC{\text{ fives}}} = \greenD{40}$
$\fbox{\greenD{40}}\div\purpleC{5} = \blueD{8}$ $~~$ because  $~~$ $\blueD8\times\purpleC5=\fbox{\greenD{40}}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What number makes both equations true?**

$\begin{align}
6\times  \blue\square~&=30\\\\
30\div6&=\blue\square
\end{align}$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD6$ groups, how many would need to be in each group to make a total of $\greenD{30}$?

There are $\blueD6$ groups of $\purpleC5$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5}+\purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5}}_{\blueD{6}\purpleC{\text{ fives}}} = \greenD{30}$

$\begin{align}
6\times  \purpleC5~&=30\\\\
30\div6&=\purpleC5
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What number makes both equations true?**

$\begin{align}
4\times  \blue\square~&=16\\\\
16\div4&=\blue\square
\end{align}$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD4$ groups, how many would need to be in each group to make a total of $\greenD{16}$?

There are $\blueD4$ groups of $\purpleC4$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{4} + \purpleC{4} + \purpleC{4} + \purpleC{4}}_{\blueD{4}\purpleC{\text{ fours}}} = \greenD{16}$

$\begin{align}
4\times  \purpleC4~&=16\\\\
16\div4&=\purpleC4
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What number makes both equations true?**

$\begin{align}
\blue\square\times 5~&=35\\\\
35\div\blue\square &= 5
\end{align}$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
How many groups of $\purpleC5$ do we need to make a total of $\greenD{35}$?

There are $\blueD7$ groups of $\purpleC5$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5}+ \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5} + \purpleC{5}}_{\blueD{7}\purpleC{\text{ fives}}} = \greenD{35}$

$\begin{align}
\blueD7\times 5~&=35\\\\
35\div\blueD7&=5
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What number makes both equations true?**

$\begin{align}
\blue\square\times 3~&=3\\\\
3\div\blue\square &= 3
\end{align}$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
How many groups of $\purpleC3$ do we need to make a total of $\greenD{3}$?

There is $\blueD1$ group of $\purpleC3$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{3}}_{\blueD{1}\purpleC{\text{ three}}} = \greenD{3}$

$\begin{align}
\blueD1\times 3~&=3\\\\
3\div\blueD1&=3
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What number makes both equations true?**

$\begin{align}
\blue\square\times 7~&=63\\\\
63\div\blue\square &= 7
\end{align}$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
How many groups of $\purpleC7$ do we need to make a total of $\greenD{63}$?

There are $\blueD9$ groups of $\purpleC7$.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{7} + \purpleC{7} + \purpleC{7} + \purpleC{7}+ \purpleC{7} + \purpleC{7} + \purpleC{7}+ \purpleC{7} + \purpleC{7}}_{\blueD{9}\purpleC{\text{ sevens}}} = \greenD{63}$

$\begin{align}
\blueD9\times 7~&=63\\\\
63\div\blueD9&=7
\end{align}$

3\times8&=~\blue\square\\\\
\blue\square\div8 &= 3
\end{align}$

hints:

\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD3$ groups of $\purpleC8$, how many total do we have?

There are $\greenD{24}$ total.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8} + \purpleC{8}}_{\blueD{3}\purpleC{\text{ eights}}} = \greenD{24}$

$\begin{align}
3\times 8~&=\greenD{24}\\\\
\greenD{24}\div8&=3
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What number makes both equations true?**

$\begin{align}
3\times9&=~\blue\square\\\\
\blue\square\div9 &= 3
\end{align}$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can relate multiplication and division:


$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{Number of groups}}&\times\purpleC{\text{ size of the group}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
\purpleC{\text{Size of the group}}&\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} =\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}\\\\
 \greenD{\text{Total}}&\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} 
\end{align}$
If we have $\blueD3$ groups of $\purpleC9$, how many total do we have?

There are $\greenD{27}$ total.

$\underbrace{\purpleC{9}+\purpleC{9}+\purpleC{9}}_{\blueD{3}\purpleC{\text{ nines}}} = \greenD{27}$

$\begin{align}
3\times 9~&=\greenD{27}\\\\
\greenD{27}\div9&=3
\end{align}$
